Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often try to work through problems physically or practice things rather than just reading. I'd usually carve out chunks of time when I felt motivated, maybe around 14 hours a week total, but it wasn't like a set schedule. Some weeks I'd be really into it, other weeks I'd barely touch the material until something forced me to.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was coming up. If there was a deadline looming or an exam approaching, I'd suddenly be all in, studying intensely. But during quieter periods, I'd let things slide and focus on other stuff in my life. I never really had that disciplined 'study every Tuesday at 3pm' kind of routine - it just didn't work with how my brain operates.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or just wanting to check if anything new had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what was happening in the discussion forums, since I did participate there a fair bit. If I got an email notification about something, that would prompt me to jump on. Other times I'd log in because I was feeling anxious about falling behind and wanted to reassure myself.
Q4: Yeah, there was a period mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, grab coffee, and check the LMS before doing anything else. That happened after I started engaging more with the discussion posts - it became kind of social in a way, even though I didn't really interact with peers directly. The CBT sessions I did helped me build better habits around that too.
Q5: The video content was probably most useful for me because I could watch demonstrations and actually see how things worked. I spent a decent amount of time on those - around 29 minutes per session usually. The discussion forums were also helpful because writing out my thoughts forced me to actually process the material. Anything that let me engage actively rather than just passively read was gold for my learning style.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They just didn't click with how I learn best. I'd skim them at most, or download them with good intentions and never open them again. The more text-heavy resources felt like a chore, and when my motivation was already low, I just couldn't push myself to engage with them properly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to what I'd be tested on or if it was genuinely interesting to work through. Anything that felt like busywork or checkbox-ticking, I'd mentally check out. I also valued activities where I could actually do something - practice problems, interactive elements, discussions where I could articulate my thinking. Passive consumption never felt productive to me.
Q8: Optional activities always felt lower priority, like they were nice-to-haves rather than essentials. With required ones, there was this external pressure that helped me actually complete them, even when motivation was low. Optional stuff I'd tell myself I'd get to later, and later never came. The stress of requirements actually helped me stay on track in a weird way.
Q9: Oh absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and work through them on paper, making my own notes and diagrams. I'd also look up YouTube videos that explained concepts in different ways - sometimes a different perspective just clicks better. A lot of my actual learning happened with a notebook in front of me, working through problems by hand, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were whole study sessions where I'd have printed materials spread across my desk, working through practice problems manually, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with friends sometimes - we'd quiz each other verbally or work through concepts on a whiteboard. That collaborative learning was huge for me but completely invisible digitally. My best understanding often came from these offline moments.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once online, then download the slides or transcript and review them multiple times offline. Or I'd access a problem set, print it out, and spend hours working through it on paper. The initial access was just the starting point - the real engagement happened afterward, away from the screen.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. My stress levels would spike and I'd feel this moderate anxiety that made it hard to focus. Sometimes I'd just feel burned out and the thought of logging in felt overwhelming. Those were the times my attendance and assignment completion really suffered, even though I was still trying to keep up mentally.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS - maybe log in, glance at what was due, and log out without really engaging. Competing responsibilities pulled my attention away constantly. The workload sometimes felt crushing, and confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections entirely rather than face feeling stupid. It's not great, but that's honestly how it went.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, or I'd scroll through readings without absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd log in and just... stare at the screen, feeling too mentally drained to actually process anything. The activity was there in the logs, but real engagement? Not so much during those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was low motivation - when you're not feeling it, it's so easy to say 'tomorrow.' Part of it was anxiety too; sometimes starting felt harder than the actual work. I'd avoid things that seemed difficult or unclear because facing that challenge felt too daunting in the moment. My assignment completion rate really shows that pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become incredibly productive, cramming everything in at the last minute. Without that pressure, I'd drift. The LMS deadline reminders were actually helpful - they'd jolt me into action. But it also meant my engagement was really spiky rather than consistent throughout the course.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But when feedback was delayed or absent, I'd kind of lose interest in that area. Knowing how I was doing helped me calibrate my efforts. The feedback on my exam scores was encouraging since I did reasonably well there, which kept me going even when other things were slipping.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework, but it didn't always match how I naturally learn. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but the pacing sometimes felt off for me. When things were well-organized with clear expectations, I engaged more. When it felt scattered or unclear, I'd disengage and try to figure things out on my own terms.
Q19: The discussion forums being active made engagement easier - I liked having that outlet. But some of the assignment structures felt rigid and didn't accommodate different learning approaches well. Video content was great for me as a kinesthetic learner when it showed demonstrations. Text-heavy modules with no interactive elements were harder to get through and I often skipped large portions.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week - logging in repeatedly, clicking around, maybe rewatching videos, but never actually getting to the submission point. High activity might reflect anxiety or confusion rather than productive engagement. You might be searching for understanding, trying different resources, but still not feeling confident enough to submit work. The activity shows effort, just not completion.
Q21: That actually does reflect my experience somewhat. My exam score was pretty good even though my attendance and assignment completion were low. I did a lot of learning offline - studying from downloaded materials, working through problems on paper, using external resources. The LMS activity doesn't capture that at all. Someone can understand the material deeply without it showing in click data.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you're clicking on, time spent on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually understanding or just going through motions. They miss offline study, mental engagement during passive viewing, and the emotional state behind the numbers. A login doesn't equal learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious, asking questions, making connections to other things I know. For me, engagement is active - it's working through problems, discussing ideas, applying concepts. It's not just being present in the system; it's being mentally invested in learning something meaningful.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the CBT sessions helped me manage my stress and stay in the game when things got tough. It wouldn't capture my offline study habits, the physical notes I made, or the genuine interest I developed in certain topics. It also wouldn't show that despite my low motivation scores and inconsistent patterns, I actually did learn and earn a Merit. The numbers tell one story, but my actual experience was more complex and ultimately more positive than the data might suggest.
